content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 25, 1991


Deborah A. Churton
Counsel, East Palo Alto
Redevelopment Agency
Ware and Freidenrich
400 Hamilton Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94301-1825

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-321

Dear Ms. Churton:

     This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on
behalf of East Palo Alto Planning Commissioners Melvin Harris,
Foster Curry, Joseph Goodwill and Tony Franco concerning their
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your request concerns a series of
decisions involved in the establishment of a redevelopment project
area, we are providing you with general guidelines applicable to
the decisions generally.² Please note that nothing in this letter
should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken
place.

QUESTIONS

     1. May East Palo Alto Planning Commissioners participate in
decisions concerning the Gateway Redevelopment Project if the
decisions may directly and indirectly affect their real property
interests?


_________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

     ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 2


     2. Will the decisions on the redevelopment project affect
the public generally in the same manner as the planning
commissioners pursuant to the exceptions in the Act?

     3. If a majority of planning commissioners are disqualified
due to financial interests which will be affected by the decision,
may some of the officials be permitted to participate throughout
the series of decisions concerning the redevelopment area?

                         CONCLUSIONS

     1. East Palo Alto Planning Commissioners may not participate
in any decisions which will have a foreseeable and material affect
on any of their economic interests.

     2. The "public generally" exception will not apply unless
the decisions will affect a significant segment of East Palo Alto
in the same manner as the decisions will affect the interests of
the planning commissioners. This does not appear to be the case
here.

     3. Where a quorum cannot be achieved due to
disqualifications, a disqualified planning commissioner may be
legally required to participate despite such disqualification. A
planning commissioner may participate if he or she is selected at
random to achieve a quorum. If necessary, more than one planning
commissioner may be selected at random to achieve the quorum.

     Once selected, the same planning commissioner or
commissioners may participate in all interrelated decisions.
Other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified.
Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for some
other reason, other planning commissioners may not be substituted
for the commissioner chosen randomly to achieve the quorum. If
the subject matter of the decision is unchanged as are the
disqualifying interests, there is no basis to repeat the random
selection process.

                           FACTS

     The City of East Palo Alto is currently in the process of
implementing a series of redevelopment projects to increase the
city's tax base and enhance the city's property values. East Palo
Alto covers two and one-half square miles and has a population of
approximately 23,500 persons. Aside from a few retail
establishments, the city is primarily a residential community
comprised of single and multi-family residential units.

     The Gateway Area is intended to be developed into a full-
scale, super-regional retail mall (the "project"). The project
area covers approximately 115 acres and is currently zoned for
single and multi-family residential and some commercial uses. The
major goal of the project would be to establish the Gateway Area
as the retail center of the community.

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 3


You stated that the planning commission will be involved in a
series of decisions concerning the redevelopment project in the
near future, including the review of an environmental impact
report, general plan amendment, specific plan, rezoning amendment,
development plan, and, eventually, a planned use development
permit application, tentative tract map and development agreement.
You have become concerned that because some of your planning
commissioners have property and business interests in and near the
project area, they may have conflicts of interest with respect to
the redevelopment project.

You stated that Planning Commissioner Harris owns two single
family residences within approximately 300 feet of the project
area. One serves as his principal residence, and the other is
leased out. Commissioner Curry owns a single family residence
serving as his principal residence within 300 feet of the project
area. In addition, during the last 12 months, Commissioner Curry
has received $900 in exchange for his management of rental
property located within the project area. The office of the
income owns property in the project area. Commissioner Goodwill
owns four pieces of commercial property within 1,000 to 1,500 feet
of the project area and his daughter, his dependent child, owns a
piece of commercial property also within 1,000 to 1,500 feet of
the project area. Commissioner Goodwill and his daughter lease
the parcels to tenants for retail/commercial uses. While the
project anticipates retail use, you stated that the type of retail
businesses currently planned would not compete with existing
small-scale commercial and retail uses in the city. Finally,
Commissioner Franco owns and operates a security alarm
installation business which is situated on property he leases in
the project area. Some of his customers own commercial and
residential property within the project area.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act (the "Act"), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, would
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A "public official" is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation 18700 as every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. This definition would include members of the
East Palo Alto Planning Commission.

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 4


1.  Economic Interests

    Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

         (a) Any business entity in which the public
    official has a direct or indirect investment worth
    one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

         (b) Any real property in which the public
    official has a direct or indirect interest worth
    one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

         (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
    and other than loans by a commercial lending
    institution in the regular course of business on
    terms available to the public without regard to
    official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
    dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
    received by or promised to the public official
    within 12 months prior to the time when the
    decision is made.

         (d) Any business entity in which the public
    official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
    employee, or holds any position of management.

                        Section 87103(a)-(d).

    According to your facts, Planning Commissioners Harris,
Curry, Goodwill and Franco all have a variety of economic
interests³ which may be foreseeably and materially affected by the
redevelopment decisions.

    The interests of the planning commissioners are as follows:

1.  Planning Commissioner Harris

    A.  A single family residence within 300 feet.

    B.  A single family residence within 300 feet which is leased
out.

    C.  The lessee is also a source of income.


³ Please be aware that where any one economic interest of an
official will be foreseeably and materially affected by a
decision, the official may not participate in the decision.

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 5


2.  Commissioner Curry

    A.  A single family residence within 300 feet.

    C.  A source of income owning property in the project area.

3.  Commissioner Goodwill

    A.  Four commercial properties within 1,000 to 1,500 feet.

    B.  An additional commercial property within 1,000 to 1,500
feet owned by his dependent child.

    C.  Five retail-business lessees (sources of income)
operating businesses on the property within 1,000 to 1,500 feet.

4.  Commissioner Franco

    A.  A leasehold interest in the project area.

    B.  A security alarm business in the project area.

    C.  Customers in the project area.

    Thus, each planning commissioner is required to disqualify
himself from making or participating in any decision which would
have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the official's
economic interest that is distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally.

2.  Foreseeability

    Whether the financial consequences of a decision are
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.  An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.  Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.  (Downey Cares v. Downey Community
Development Commission (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 938; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

    The anticipated result of any redevelopment plan is to
increase the property values and improve the business climate
within the project area.  The very nature of redevelopment
projects has led the Commission to find that it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a financial effect on real property
values and business interests located within or near project
areas.  (In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Armento Advice
Letter, No. A-90-499.)  Since each planning commissioner has
economic interests in or near the project area, we presume that a
financial effect on each is foreseeable.

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 6


3.  Materiality

     However, disqualification is only required where the
foreseeable effect on a planning commissioner's economic interest
is also material. The Commission has adopted a series of
regulations which provide guidance concerning whether the
foreseeable financial effects of a decision are material.
(Regulation 18702.) These regulations apply different standards
depending on whether the decision will directly or indirectly
affect the official's economic interest. Thus, we must consider
each interest of the planning commissioners individually to
determine if disqualification is required.

     A.  Real Property

     Planning Commissioner Franco leases property in the
redevelopment area for his alarm business. Both Planning
Commissioners Harris and Curry own real property within 300 feet
of the project area. Planning Commissioner Goodwill owns property
within 1,000 to 1,500 feet of the project area which he leases to
commercial businesses.

     If Planning Commissioner Franco's leasehold interest in the
redevelopment area is for a term greater than one month to month, it
is considered a real property interest under the Act. (Section
82033; Regulation 18233.) Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(D) provides
that a decision's effect on an economic interest is deemed to be
material and disqualification is required if:

          (D)  The decision is to designate the survey
     area, to select the project area, to adopt the
     preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
     to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
     redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
     redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
     the above decisions; and real property in which the
     official has an interest, or any part of it is
     located within the boundaries (or the proposed
     boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

                              Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(D),
                              emphasis added.

     Thus, the decision to establish a redevelopment area that
encompasses the property Planning Commissioner Franco leases is
deemed to have a material financial effect on his real property
and Planning Commissioner Franco may not participate.

     Regulation 18702.3(a)(1) provides the effect of a decision on
the real property interest of a public official which is within

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 7


300 feet of the redevelopment project area⁴ is material unless the
decision will have no financial effect upon the official's real
property interests.

Planning Commissioner Harris owns two pieces of real property
which are within 300 feet of the redevelopment project area and
Planning Commissioner Curry owns a personal residence which is
within 300 feet of the project area. Because of the close
proximity of Planning Commissioners Harris' and Curry's real
property to the subject property, there is a presumption that
financial effect on the real property will be material. This
presumption may be rebutted only by a showing that there will be
no financial effect on either planning commissioners real property
interests. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(1); Phelps Advice Letter, No.
A-88-429.)

You have provided us with no facts to indicate that the real
property of Planning Commissioners Harris and Curry will not be
financially affected by the decisions concerning the redevelopment
project. Absent such information we conclude that Planning
Commissioners Harris and Curry real property interests will be
materially affected by the decision.

Planning Commissioner Goodwill's property is 1,000 to 1,500
feet from the project area. Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) provides
that the effect of a decision is material as to real property if:

(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will have
a reasonably foreseeable effect of:

(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or


___________________
⁴ The boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment
project area are the "boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision" wherever the
decision is a redevelopment decision to designate the survey area,
to make findings of blight, to select the project area, to adopt
the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify
the environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to
add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend
any of the above decisions. (Regulation 18702.3(e).)

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 8


     (B)  Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.

     Consistent with the foreseeability discussion in the Ogelsby
Opinion, the effect of the redevelopment project on property
adjacent to the project will be a substantial increase in the
property's value.  We cannot definitively determine whether the
magnitude of the financial effect on the property interests of
Planning Commissioner Goodwill caused by the redevelopment project
is disqualifying.  We must leave this factual determination of
dollar-effect to you and Planning Commissioner Goodwill within the
guidelines provided by Regulation 18702.3.

     You also stated that Planning Commissioner Goodwill's
daughter owns a piece of property which is leased for commercial
use and is located the same distance from the redevelopment
project area.  You stated the daughter is a dependent child.
Section 87103 provides that property owned by an official's
immediate family is a disqualifying economic interest of the
official, because the official would have an "indirect interest"
in the property.  "Immediate family" is defined in Section 82029
as the official's spouse and dependent children.5  Thus, the
property of the planning commissioner's daughter is treated as the
planning commissioner's property under the Act.

     B.  Sources of Income

     Planning Commissioners Curry and Franco also have sources of
income in the project area.  Planning Commissioner Harris has a
source of income within 300 feet of the project area.  Planning
Commissioner Goodwill has sources of income which are business
entities within 1,000 to 1,500 feet of the redevelopment project
area.

     Where a source of income is not directly before the planning
commission, as is the case here, but may be indirectly affected by
a planning commission decision, Regulations 18702.2 and 18702.6
apply.  Whether the indirect effect on a business-entity source of
income is material depends on the financial size of the business
entity.  For example, if Planning Commissioner Goodwill's lessees
are relatively small business entities, Regulation 18702.2(g)


_____________________________

     5 A child is considered a "dependent child" for purposes of the
Act if the child is under 18 and the elected official is entitled
to claim the dependent child as such for income tax purposes.
(Tremlett Advice Letter, No. I-89-386.)

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 9


provides that for such small business entities the indirect effect
of a decision is material where:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3) The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

     Consequently, assuming that subdivision (g) is the
appropriate standard, and the redevelopment decisions will affect
the gross revenues of the businesses that lease Planning
Commissioner Goodwill's property by $10,000 or more for a fiscal
year, or the value of the lessees' assets or liabilities by
$10,000 or more, or will result in the lessees incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more,
Planning Commissioner Goodwill may not participate.

     Where a source of income is an individual, as appears to be
the case with Planning Commissioners Harris and Curry, and the
individual is not directly before the planning commission but may
be affected indirectly, Regulation 18702.6 provides:

     The effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is a source of income or gifts to an
official if any of the following applies:

          (a) The decision will affect the
individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities
(other than real property) by $1,000 or more;
or

          (b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered material under
Section 18702.3 or Section 18702.4.

     Thus, Planning Commissioner Harris must disqualify himself if
the source of income to him will be affected by $1,000 or if the
source of income has a property interest in or near the project
area. Planning Commissioner Curry's source of income owns

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 10


property in the redevelopment project area. Thus, Planning
Commissioner Curry's source of income is also disqualifying with
respect to the decision in question.

    C.  Business Interests

    Finally, Planning Commissioner Franco's economic interest in
his alarm business will be disqualifying if the redevelopment
decision will affect his business by the dollar amounts set forth
in the appropriate provision of Regulation 18702.2, as discussed
above.

4.  "Public Generally" Exception

    Public officials with financial interests that will be
materially affected by a decision may participate in the decision
if the effect on their property is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. For the "public generally"
exception to apply, a decision must affect the official's
interests in substantially the same manner as it would affect a
significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18703.)

    The "public" consists of the entire jurisdiction of the
agency in questions. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) This is
so because all the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents
of the official. Thus, the planning commissioners may
participate in the redevelopment decisions if the effect on the
planning commissioners financial interests is substantially the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the population of
the City of East Palo Alto.

    According to your facts, the project area covers 180 acres of
the city's 2.5 square miles of area (1,500 acres). Thus, the area
covered by the project is approximately 12 percent of the city.
You also stated that of the 3,410 single-family residences in the
city, 241 are located in or within 300 feet of the project area.
This equates to approximately 7 percent of the city's single
family residences.

    The Commission has never adopted a strict arithmetic formula
for determining what constitutes a significant segment of the
public. However, in order to apply the "public generally"
exception, the affected population must be large and heterogeneous
in nature. (in re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62.) While the
population affected appears to be heterogeneous in nature, we do
not believe that 7 percent of the single family residences in the
city or 12 percent of the area of the city is sufficiently large

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 11


to invoke the exception.6 Thus, it is unlikely the "public
generally" exception would permit Planning Commissioners Harris
and Curry to participate in the decision since a relative small
portion of the population in the city would be affected in the
same manner as the planning commissioners.

Moreover, you described the area as currently zoned for
single and multi-family residential and commercial uses. Thus, it
is unlikely that the area constitutes the core of commercial
business in the city. (See e.g., In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops
77.) Therefore, the exception would not permit Commissioner
Franco to participate despite his business interest in the project
area. Similarly, it appears that despite the fact that the
businesses inside the project area will not compete with Planning
Commissioner Goodwill's lessees, the lessee's will still benefit
greatly from the redevelopment of the area adjacent to their
business and will be affected differently from other businesses
more distant. Consequently, in each case, the planning
commissioners' economic interests will be affected in a manner
different that the rest of the population of East Palo Alto.

In addition to the traditional "public generally" exception
in the Act, however, Regulation 18703.1 also provides a "public
generally" exception for small jurisdictions. Regulation 18703.1
provides that under certain specified circumstances the effect of
a governmental decision on a public official's principal residence
is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally in
small jurisdictions. However, the exception is only available
with respect to the public official's interest in his or her
principal residence as follows:

(a) The effect of a governmental decision on
the principal residence of a public official is
not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally where all of the following conditions are
met:

(1) The public official's agency has
jurisdiction over a population of 25,000 or
less, covering a geographic area of ten square
miles or less.


6 The "public generally" exception is an exception to the general
rules requiring disqualification on the basis of an official's
economic interests. It is a fundamental rule of statutory
construction that an exception to a general rule is to be narrowly
construed. (Julius Goldman's Egg City v. Air Pollution Control
Dept. of Ventura County (1981) 116 Cal.App.3d 741.) As such, the
narrow interpretation of the exception is preferred.

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 12


(2) The public official is required to
reside within the jurisdiction.

(3) The public official, if he or she is
an elected officer has been elected in an at-
large election.

(4) The decision does not have a direct
effect (as provided in Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)) on the public official's
principal residence.

(5) The principal residence is more than
100 feet from the boundaries of the property
which is the subject of the decision.

(6) The principal residence is located
on a parcel of land not more than one-quarter
acre in size or, alternatively, a residential
lot not larger than 125 percent of the median
residential lot size for the jurisdiction.

* * *

(c) Even if the above conditions are not met,
the effect of a governmental decision on the
principal residence of a public official may be
determined not to be distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally in accordance with
Regulation 18703 or any other regulations of the
Commission not applicable solely to small
jurisdictions.

While the characteristics of your jurisdiction do fall within
the scope of the regulation, the exception would not apply to
Planning Commissioners Goodwill and Franco if they are
disqualified due to business interests or sources of income.
(Regulation 18703.1(a).) Nor would the exception apply to the
real property interests of Planning Commissioners Harris and Curry
since both own residences within 300 feet of the project area.
(Regulation 18703.1(a)(5).)

5. Legally Required Participation

Section 87101 provides a limited exception where an otherwise
disqualified planning commissioner may participate if his
participation is legally required:

Section 87100 does not prevent any public
official from making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision to the extent his

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 13


participation is legally required for the action or
decision to be made.

Section 87101.

This exception has been narrowly interpreted to permit the
participation of the fewest financially interested persons
possible in any decision. (In re Hudson (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 13;
Hill Advice Letter, No. I-89-160.) Consequently, Regulation 18701
provides in pertinent part:

(a) A public official is not legally required
to make or to participate in the making of a
governmental decision within the meaning of
Government Code Section 87101 unless there exists
no alternative source of decision consistent with
the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing
the decision.

* * *

(c) This regulation shall be construed
narrowly, and shall:

(1) Not be construed to permit an
official, who is otherwise disqualified under
Government Code Section 87100, to vote to
break a tie.

(2) Not be construed to allow a member
of any public agency, who is otherwise
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, to vote if a quorum can be convened of
other members of the agency who are not
disqualified under Government Code Section
87100, whether or not such other members are
actually present at the time of the
disqualification.

Thus, where only a single member is needed to make a quorum,
only one disqualified member is permitted to participate in the
decision. (Skousen Advice Letter, No. A-88-162; Martin Advice
Letter, No. I-88-375.) This is because "[T]he purposes of the Act
are best served by a rule which minimizes participation in
government decisions by officials with a conflict of interest."
(In re Hudson, supra.)

You have asked whether various decisions concerning the
redevelopment project may be treated as separate decisions; or,
whether a new drawing of lots for each decision is required.
Where there are changed circumstances (either as to the official's

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 14


personal financial interests or as to the proposed decision) which
alter the conclusion as to disqualification of any councilmember,
a new situation exists requiring a fresh look at the situation
and, if appropriate, a new drawing of lots. (Hopkins Advice
Letter, No. A-82-088.)

However an agency is not required to repeatedly reselect the
participants in a series of related decisions. (Hopkins Advice
Letter, supra.) An agency may utilize the same drawing by lot if
the decision is really a continuation of the same issue or
interrelated. Moreover, under some circumstances, it would be
improper to repeat the random selection where the subject matter
of the decision is unchanged, as are the disqualifying interests.
(Hill Advice Letter, No. A-87-110.)

6. Segmentation of Decision

You stated that the planning commission would be confronted
with a series of decisions concerning the redevelopment project in
the near future, including the consideration of an environmental
impact report, general plan amendment, specific plan, rezoning
amendment and redevelopment plan. In addition, if the
redevelopment project is approved, the planning commission will be
required to consider a planned use development permit application,
tentative tract map and development agreement. For purposes of
this letter we are assuming that the decisions are project-wide
decisions.

It would appear from your facts, however, that as the
redevelopment decisions move forward there may be some other
decisions which will not independently affect some of the planning
commissions' various interests and may be severable from the
major decisions for which the planning commissioners were
disqualified. Thus, while the conclusions in this letter apply to
most of the major redevelopment decisions, a conflict of interest
may not exist for a specific planning commissioner with respect to
future separable decisions. Therefore we are providing a general
discussion of segmentation of decisions for your information.

Under many circumstances a series of decisions may be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.) For example, if it is determined that an official
has a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a specific
project because of the project's impact on an economic interest,
the official may be similarly disqualified as to decisions
concerning the financing of that project. The reason is that the
decisions concerning the financing of the project could in fact
alter the previous decision for which the official was
disqualified. (Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038.)

Our File No. I-91-321
Page 15


Conversely, where the decisions are separable, such as
smaller projects in a larger project, and none affects the
decisions on the other projects, each project may be analyzed
separately to determine if the official has a conflict of
interest.  (Kilian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522.)

As stated above, it would appear from your facts that as the
redevelopment decisions move forward they may be separable.  If
indeed the decisions are separable and a planning commissioner
determines he has a conflict of interest with respect to some, but
not all the decisions, the following procedure should be followed
to permit the planning commissioner to participate:

     (1)  The decisions for which the planning commissioner has a
disqualifying financial interest should be segregated from the
other decisions.

     (2)  The decisions from which the planning commissioner is
disqualified should be considered first, and a final decision
reached by the planning commission without the planning
commissioner participating in any way.

     (3)  Once a decision has been made on the projects for which
the planning commissioner has a disqualifying financial interest,
he may participate in the deliberations regarding the other
issues, so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any way affect the decisions from which he was
disqualified.  (Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)

We do not have sufficient information on each decision to
come before the planning commission to advise on segmentation.
Please contact us if you believe this analysis may be applicable
to a specific decision.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   By:  John W. Wallace
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

_________________________

S
1. East PaIo AIto Planning commissioners nay not participate
in any decisions which r.riII have a foreseeable and material affect
on any of their econonic interests.
2. The ttpublic generally'r exception vill not apply unless

here .

disqualifications, 
a disqualified planning commissioner nay be
tegitty required to participate despite such disqualification. 
A
planning co:nnissioner may participate if he or she is selected at
iandon io achieve a quorum. If necessary, more than one planning
conmissioner may be selected at random to achieve the quorum.
once selected, the same pJ.anning conmissioner or
conmissioners may particiPate in all interrelated decisions.
other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified.
Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for sone
other reason, other planning conmissioners may not be substituted
for the cornmissioner chosen randomly to achieve the quorum. If
the subject matter of the decision is unchanged as are the

selection process.

sections.analysis:
The Political Reforn Act (the trActrr), was enacted by the
people of the State of California by initiative 
in 1974' The
purpose for the conf I ict-o f- interest provisions of the Act was to
ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would
perf orrn their duties in an impartial manner, free fron bias caused
by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any publ j,c officiat 
frorn naking, participating in
naking, or otherwise using his or her official 
position to
influence a governnental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest. 
A "public official" 
is defined in Section
82048 and Regulation L8700 as every natural person who is a
menber, officer, 
ernployee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. This definition r.rould include nernbers of the
East PaIo Alto Planning Comnission.

-32■

Section 87103 specifies that an official 
has a financial
interest within the neaning of Section 8710o if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a Daterial financial
effect, distinguishable fron the effect on the public generally,
on the official 
or a roenber of his or her inmediate family or on:
(a) Any business entity in nhich the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investnent lrorth
one thousand dollars ($1,ooo1 or more.

official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.

and other than loans by a connercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tlro hundred fifty
dollars (S25o) or nore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the time lrhen the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, Partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any Position of management.

o f-tirE decision is unchanged, as are the disqualifying interests.
(HiII Advice Letter, No. A-87-110.)
6. Seqmentation of Decis ion
You stated that the planning cornmission would be confronted
with a series of decisions concerning the redevelopnent project in
the near future, including the consideration of an environnental'
irnpact report, general PIan anendment, specific pJ'an, rezoning
arnendment and redeveloprnent pIan. In addition, if the
redevelopment project is approved, the planning conmission.will be
required to coirsider a planned use deveJ.opment permit application,
tentative tract nap and development agreement. For purposes of
this letter ere are assuming that the decisions are project-wide
decisions.
It $/ould appear fron your facts, however, that as the
redevelopnent decisions move forsard there may be sone other
decision! r.rhich wiIl not independently affect some of the planning
commissioners' various interests and may be severable from the
rnajor decisions for which the planning conmissioners were
disquatified. 
Thus. while the conclusions in this letter apply to
rnos€ of the najor redevelopment decisions, a conflict of interest
nay not exist for a specific planning connissioner with respect to
future separable decisions. Therefore lre are providing a general
discussion of segmentation of decisions for your information.
Under many circumstances a series of decisions rnay be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (MiIler Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.) For exampJ,e, if it is deterrnined that an official
has a conflict of interest as to decisions concerning a specific
project because of the project's i:npact on an economic interest,
the official 
may be sinilarly disqualified as to decisions
concerning the financing of that project. 
The reason is that the
decisions concerning the financing of the project could in fact
alter the previous decisi.on for vhich the officiaL was
disqualified. 
(Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038.)


conversely, where the decisions are separable, such as
small.er projects in a larger project, and none affects the
decisioni oi trre other projects, each project may be analyzed
separately to determine if the officiaL has a conflict of
interest. 
(Kilian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522. )
As stated above, it would appear from your facts that as the
redevelopnent decisions move forward they may be separable. rf
indeed tire decisions are separable and a pJ.anning commissioner
deternines he has a conflict of interest with respect to sone, but
not all. the decisions, the foJ'lowing procedure should be folloued
to pernit the planning com:nissioner to participate:
(1) The decisions for shich the planning comrnissioner. has a
disqualifying f j.nancial interest should be segregated frorn the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the planning commissioner is
aisqualified shoul.d be considered first, 
and a final decision
."a6hed by the planning cornmission without the planning
commissioner participating in any way.
(3) once a decision has been made on the projects for which
the pianning commissioner has a disqualj.fying financial interest,
he rnly participate in the deliberations regarding the. other
issuei,- so long as those deliberations do not result in a
reopening or in any uay affect the decisions fron which he was
disguaLiiied. 
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)
we do not have sufficient inforrnation on each deci.sion to
come before the pJ.anning comrnission to advise on segmentation'
Pl.ease contact u! if you believe this analysis may be applicable
to a specific decision.

CONCLUSION: S
1. East PaIo AIto Planning commissioners nay not participate
in any decisions which r.riII have a foreseeable and material affect
on any of their econonic interests.
2. The ttpublic generally'r exception vill not apply unless
the decisions wiII affect a significant segment of East Palo Alto
in the sane nanner as the decisions will affect the interests of

A
planning co:nnissioner may participate if he or she is selected at

once selected, the same pJ.anning conmissioner or

other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified.
Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for sone

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission July 25, 1991 Deborah A. Churton Counsel, East Palo Alto Redevelopment Agency Ware and Freidenrich 400 Hamilton Avenue Palo Alto, CA 94301-1825 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-91-321 Dear Ms. Churton: This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on behalf of East Palo Alto Planning Commissioners Melvin Harris, Foster Curry, Joseph Goodwill and Tony Franco concerning their duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your request concerns a series of decisions involved in the establishment of a redevelopment project area, we are providing you with general guidelines applicable to the decisions generally.² Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. QUESTIONS 1. May East Palo Alto Planning Commissioners participate in decisions concerning the Gateway Redevelopment Project if the decisions may directly and indirectly affect their real property interests? _________________________ ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660 Our File No. I-91-321 Page 2 2. Will the decisions on the redevelopment project affect the public generally in the same manner as the planning commissioners pursuant to the exceptions in the Act? 3. If a majority of planning commissioners are disqualified due to financial interests which will be affected by the decision, may some of the officials be permitted to participate throughout the series of decisions concerning the redevelopment area? CONCLUSIONS 1. East Palo Alto Planning Commissioners may not participate in any decisions which will have a foreseeable and material affect on any of their economic interests. 2. The "public generally" exception will not apply unless the decisions will affect a significant segment of East Palo Alto in the same manner as the decisions will affect the interests of the planning commissioners. This does not appear to be the case here. 3. Where a quorum cannot be achieved due to disqualifications, a disqualified planning commissioner may be legally required to participate despite such disqualification. A planning commissioner may participate if he or she is selected at random to achieve a quorum. If necessary, more than one planning commissioner may be selected at random to achieve the quorum. Once selected, the same planning commissioner or commissioners may participate in all interrelated decisions. Other disqualified planning commissioners remain disqualified. Should the commissioner chosen be unable to participate for some other reason, other planning commissioners may not be substituted for the commissioner chosen randomly to achieve the quorum. If the subject matter of the decision is unchanged as are the disqualifying
